title: TPU v1 · Coverage convergence and verification closure: three criteria answer “done and ready for tapeout?”
oneLine: Verification does not stop after enough runtime; it must produce a signable answer on whether the TPU design may proceed to synthesis and tapeout. Three conditions must all hold: functional/code coverage reaches target, every row of the 3.1 verification plan closes, and bugs converge with near-zero discovery and no unresolved severe defects. Only three green criteria pass the formal sign-off gate and release synthesis ⑦; any failure returns for hole filling.

every unhit bin waived</text></g><g class="cs-part" data-k="plan" data-g="crit"><rect class="cs-box" x="40" y="138" width="244" height="64" rx="8" fill="#f0fdf4" stroke="#86efac"/><text class="cs-t" x="162" y="164">② Verification plan closed ✓</text><text class="cs-sub" x="162" y="184">Every 3.1 feature row is verified · evidenced</text></g><g class="cs-part" data-k="bug" data-g="crit"><rect class="cs-box" x="40" y="218" width="244" height="64" rx="8" fill="#fef2f2" stroke="#fca5a5"/><text class="cs-t" x="162" y="244">③ Bugs converged ✓</text><text class="cs-sub" x="162" y="264">New-bug rate → zero · no open severe defects</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="284" y1="90" x2="368" y2="178" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><line class="cs-arr" x1="284" y1="170" x2="368" y2="186" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><line class="cs-arr" x1="284" y1="250" x2="368" y2="194" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><g class="cs-part" data-k="gate" data-g="gate"><rect class="cs-box" x="372" y="148" width="176" height="92" rx="8" fill="#eef2ff" stroke="#a5b4fc"/><text class="cs-t" x="460" y="180">Sign-off gate</text><text class="cs-sub" x="460" y="202">All three green to pass</text><text class="cs-sub" x="460" y="220">Any failure → return</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="548" y1="194" x2="624" y2="194" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><g class="cs-part" data-k="pass" data-g="pass"><rect class="cs-box" x="628" y="158" width="76" height="72" rx="8"/><text class="cs-t" x="666" y="188">Release</text><text class="cs-sub" x="666" y="208">⑦ Synthesis</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="460" y1="240" x2="460" y2="306" stroke-dasharray="5 4" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><g class="cs-part" data-k="back" data-g="back"><rect class="cs-box" x="300" y="312" width="320" height="60" rx="8"/><text class="cs-t" x="460" y="336">Return: add stimulus / waiver / fix bug + regression</text><text class="cs-sub" x="460" y="356">No release with an open criterion; never stop at “close enough”</text></g><line class="cs-arr" x1="300" y1="342" x2="232" y2="282" stroke-dasharray="5 4" marker-end="url(#csar)"/><text class="cs-note" x="40" y="304">Note: coverage/plan/bug closure is standard practice, not TPU's published metrics</text></svg>

legend.name: Criterion ① Coverage target reached (coverage closure)
legend.desc: Both functional coverage—covergroup hits for K∈{1,64,128,256}, full/partial arrays, accumulator overflow/saturation, legal/illegal/back-to-back dozen-plus CISC sequences, and CDC phases—and code coverage—line, branch, condition, toggle, and FSM states/transitions—must close. Every unhit bin receives stimulus or a documented waiver. Coverage answers whether a scenario was exercised, not how many vectors ran.

legend.name: Criterion ② Verification-plan closure
legend.desc: Return to the 3.1 feature → stimulus/coverage/criteria table and audit every row: matrix-multiply correctness, precisions/dimensions, overflow/saturation, deadlock-free handshakes, instruction sequences, and CDC. Each must be marked verified with coverage/assertion/formal evidence and satisfied acceptance criteria. A beautiful percentage cannot compensate for an open plan row.

legend.name: Criterion ③ Bug convergence
legend.desc: Evaluate the trend, not a snapshot: new-defect discovery rate must clearly approach zero and the database must contain no open severe or blocking bugs. Lower-severity issues are either fixed and regressed or have explicit disposition. Continued frequent discoveries show that design and verification have not stabilized.

legend.name: Formal sign-off gate
legend.desc: Sign-off is a review gate with explicit entry conditions, not an individual judgment. Coverage closure ✓, plan closure ✓, and bug convergence ✓ must all hold, plus an all-green regression on frozen RTL. The criteria are ANDed; any failure keeps the gate closed.

legend.name: Not met → return for closure work
legend.desc: If coverage has holes, a plan row is open, or a serious bug remains, do not release. Add directed/random stimulus, document truly unreachable waivers, fix bugs, and rerun regression. Closure discipline rejects “close enough.”

steps.cap: ① Audit coverage: are functional covergroups for sizes/corners/instructions/CDC and code coverage for line/branch/FSM transitions both closed? Hit every bin or document an approved waiver.

② Audit the plan: inspect every row in the 3.1 feature table. Each must be verified, evidenced, and at its completion criterion. Any open row is an unowned blind spot regardless of aggregate coverage.
③ Audit bugs: does discovery rate approach zero, with no open severe/blocking bugs? Frequent new defects mean the design is not stable enough for signoff.
④ Three green criteria + an all-green regression on frozen RTL → pass the formal sign-off gate. The criteria are ANDed; none is optional.
⑤ Pass → release to logic synthesis ⑦ with coverage/signoff reports. Failure → add stimulus/waivers or fix bugs, then regress again. Never stop at “close enough.”

before: Translate “done” into hard signoff criteria: define exact functional/code coverage targets—often 100% with a waiver for every unhit bin—full closure of the 3.1 plan, converged discovery rate, and zero open severe bugs. Setting them before closure prevents subjective interpretation.

Define coverage targets and waiver rules: establish functional and code thresholds, which bins can be waived, and who approves a written waiver, such as a physically unreachable array-size combination. Never invent exceptions during signoff.

Define bug severity and convergence: agree on blocking/severe/normal/minor categories, allowed open counts at signoff—usually zero severe—and a quantitative convergence rule such as zero new severe bugs across N consecutive regressions.

Define the regression baseline and RTL freeze point: all signoff evidence must come from one frozen RTL revision with specified seeds and an all-green final regression. Do not qualify an old version and then silently modify it.
Assign signoff review and ownership: specify the architecture, design, verification, and project participants and the accountable owner for each criterion. Closure is a formal, owned gate.
during: Rule: the three criteria are AND, not OR. Coverage closure, plan closure, and bug convergence must all hold. Passing coverage while one plan row or severe bug remains is false signoff; any failed criterion keeps the gate closed.

For coverage holes, add stimulus before granting waivers. Treat an unhit bin as missing stimulus by default. Add directed or constrained-random tests; waive only after proving the combination physically unreachable and documenting approval. Waivers are not a universal escape hatch.

Coverage target ≠ correctness; cross-check with the plan and assertions. 100% code coverage shows execution, not correct behavior. Functional closure must be paired with Scoreboard/Reference Model checks and SVA/formal properties that are all PROVEN. Closure requires coverage plus correctness evidence.

Judge bug convergence from a trend, not one quiet day. One or two days without discoveries may reflect missed logic. Require a stable decline versus accumulated runtime/vectors and zero open severe defects; new serious bugs mean no steady state.

Regress every change and base signoff on frozen RTL. Any RTL or stimulus change made for a bug or coverage hole triggers the relevant regression. Final evidence must come from one frozen revision, not a patchwork of results from different versions.

Scope note: coverage/plan/bug closure, all-green regression, and formal sign-off gates are standard large-ASIC practices. TPU v1's array, Unified Buffer, CISC interface, and multiple domains are public, but its private coverage targets, forms, and regression volume are not.

result.label: Three signoff criteria
result.value: Coverage at target ✓ · verification plan fully closed ✓ · bugs converged ✓

result.label: Logical relationship
result.value: All three AND all-green regression; any missing item blocks release

result.label: Coverage closure
result.value: Functional + code coverage at target; every unhit bin exercised or waived in writing

result.label: Bug convergence
result.value: New-defect rate approaches zero · no open severe/blocking bugs

result.label: Gate output
result.value: Functional-verification signoff + coverage/regression reports on frozen RTL

result.label: Release / return
result.value: Three green → synthesis ⑦; any failure → return for closure and regression

after: Archive the signoff evidence package: retain functional/code coverage reports and waivers, the plan-closure checklist, bug-convergence curves and open-defect list, and final all-green logs as traceable proof of verification.

Hand off to synthesis with constraints: deliver frozen, functionally correct RTL and coverage/signoff reports to logic synthesis ⑦. Include CDC and timing intent so downstream operates on the same signed-off version.
Enforce the freeze; changes repeat the gate: any post-signoff RTL edit invalidates affected evidence. Reassess coverage, plan rows, and regression on the new revision before updating signoff.
Reuse waiver and convergence lessons: feed waiver decisions, bug-convergence cadence, and late defect types back into future plans and coverage models to strengthen closure quality.

Document the evidence boundary: distinguish public TPU v1 structures—array, UB, CISC interface, and clock domains—from standard signoff methodology such as the three criteria, waiver workflow, and gate.
source: In-Datacenter Performance Analysis of a TPU, Jouppi et al., ISCA 2017 (the systolic array, Unified Buffer, CISC interface, and multiple clock domains are public structural facts; coverage convergence, waiver processes, and sign-off gates are standard verification practices, while TPU's exact coverage targets and environment are not public)
